content:
Of course. Here are 5 multiple-choice questions on Bus Arbitration, ranging from easy to challenging, formatted as a JSON array.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "What is the primary purpose of bus arbitration in a computer system?",
    "options": [
      "To allow multiple devices to share a single bus without conflict",
      "To increase the clock speed of the system bus",
      "To convert parallel data into serial data for transmission",
      "To provide error-checking and correction on data transfers"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "Bus arbitration is a mechanism that decides which device (e.g., processor, DMA controller, I/O device) becomes the bus master when multiple devices request control of the bus simultaneously, preventing data collisions and ensuring orderly communication."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "Which bus arbitration method uses a daisy-chain configuration where the grant signal is passed serially from one device to the next?",
    "options": [
      "Independent Request (Parallel Arbitration)",
      "Daisy-Chain Arbitration",
      "Distributed Arbitration",
      "Priority-Based Arbitration"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "Daisy-chain arbitration uses a single bus grant line that is connected through devices in a chain. The device physically closest to the arbiter gets the highest priority. If it doesn't need the bus, it passes the grant signal to the next device in the chain."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "In a system using independent request (parallel) arbitration, what is a key disadvantage compared to daisy-chain arbitration?",
    "options": [
      "It is slower due to the serial propagation of signals.",
      "It requires more bus lines, increasing system cost.",
      "It cannot assign static priorities to devices.",
      "It is more susceptible to noise and signal degradation."
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "Independent request arbitration requires a separate request and grant line for each device on the bus. While it offers faster arbitration and more flexible priority schemes, this significantly increases the number of bus lines required, which adds to the complexity and cost of the system."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "In a distributed arbitration scheme, how does a device determine if it has won control of the bus?",
    "options": [
      "It receives a dedicated grant signal from a central arbiter.",
      It compares its unique arbitration ID with the value on the shared arbitration lines; if its ID is higher, it wins.",
      "It waits for a token to be passed to it from the previous device.",
      "It monitors the bus for a specific time slot assigned to it by the system clock."
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "In distributed arbitration, there is no central arbiter. Each device monitors the shared arbitration lines. After placing its unique priority code on the lines, it compares this code to the actual value on the lines. If a higher-priority device is also requesting, it will overwrite the code. A device only assumes it has won if the code on the lines matches its own, meaning no higher-priority device asserted itself."
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "A system uses a dynamic priority arbitration algorithm where a device's priority is lowered after it is granted the bus. This is an example of which technique designed to prevent a single high-priority device from monopolizing the bus?",
    "options": [
      "Time Division Multiple Access (TDMA)",
      "Aging",
      "Priority Inversion",
      "Bus Locking"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "Aging is a technique used in arbitration algorithms to prevent starvation. It gradually increases the priority of devices that have been waiting a long time and, conversely, can decrease the priority of a device that has recently been serviced. This ensures fair access to the bus among all requesting devices over time."
  }
]
```
